message.content: <local-command-caveat>Caveat: The messages below were generated by the user while running local commands. DO NOT respond to these messages or otherwise consider them in your response unless the user explicitly asks you to.</local-command-caveat>
Show the code coverage in the readme

content.text: I'll start by checking for relevant skills and understanding the current state of the project.

input.prompt:
Find the README.md file and any existing code coverage configuration or badges. Also check if there's a Makefile with coverage commands. Look for:
1. README.md content
2. Any coverage-related files (.coveragerc, coverage.xml, etc.)
3. Makefile coverage targets
4. Any CI/CD config (.github/workflows) that runs coverage

content.content:
1→

2→ 3→

4→ 5→![Build](https://github.com/sentioxyz/releaseguard/actions/workflows/ci.yml/badge.svg) 6→![AI Co-Authored](https://img.shields.io/endpoint?url=https://gist.githubusercontent.com/Poytr1/94cc8f0ddf90bb1d04dafbb76102786d/raw/ai-commits.json) 7→ 8→## What it does 9→ 10→- **Discovers releases** by polling Docker Hub, GitHub, ECR Public, GitLab, and PyPI on configurable intervals 11→- **Routes notifications** to Slack, Discord, email, and webhooks the moment a new version lands 12→- **Generates AI reports** via Google Gemini agents that research changelogs, assess risk, and summarize what changed 13→- **Serves a dashboard** (Next.js) for managing projects, sources, subscriptions, and browsing releases in real time 14→ 15→## Architecture 16→ 17→```mermaid 18→graph LR 19→ A["Ingestion
(polling)"] --> B["PostgreSQL +
River Queue"] 20→ B --> C["Agent
(ADK-Go)"] 21→ C --> D["Routing
(channels)"] 22→ 23→ A -.- A1["Docker Hub · GitHub
ECR · GitLab · PyPI"] 24→ B -.- B1["Transactional Outbox
LISTEN/NOTIFY → SSE"] 25→ C -.- C1["Gemini LLM
research tools"] 26→ D -.- D1["Slack · Discord
Email · Webhooks"] 27→``` 28→ 29→Release insert and job enqueue happen in a single SQL transaction — zero-loss guarantee. 30→ 31→See [ARCH.md](ARCH.md), [API.md](API.md), and [DESIGN.md](DESIGN.md) for the full design. 32→ 33→## Quick start 34→ 35→**Prerequisites:** Go 1.25+, Docker, Node.js 20+ 36→ 37→```bash 38→# Start Postgres and the server (NO_AUTH mode) 39→make dev 40→ 41→# In another terminal — start the frontend 42→make frontend-install 43→make frontend-dev 44→``` 45→ 46→The API runs on `localhost:8080`, the dashboard on `localhost:3000`. 47→ 48→## Environment variables 49→ 50→| Variable | Default | Purpose | 51→|----------|---------|---------| 52→| `DATABASE_URL` | `postgres://localhost:5432/changelogue?sslmode=disable` | PostgreSQL connection | 53→| `LISTEN_ADDR` | `:8080` | HTTP server bind address | 54→| `NO_AUTH` | _(unset)_ | Set to `true` to disable API key auth (development) | 55→| `LOG_LEVEL` | `info` | Log level: `debug`, `info`, `warn`, `error` | 56→| `LLM_PROVIDER` | `gemini` | LLM provider: `gemini` or `openai` | 57→| `LLM_MODEL` | `gemini-2.5-flash` / `gpt-5.2` | Model name (default depends on provider) | 58→| `GOOGLE_API_KEY` | _(empty)_ | Gemini API key (required when `LLM_PROVIDER=gemini`) | 59→| `OPENAI_API_KEY` | _(empty)_ | OpenAI API key (required when `LLM_PROVIDER=openai`) | 60→| `OPENAI_BASE_URL` | `https://api.openai.com/v1` | OpenAI-compatible API base URL | 61→ 62→## Project structure 63→ 64→``` 65→cmd/ 66→ server/ Entry point — wires all layers together 67→ agent/ Agent CLI — run agent analysis for a project 68→internal/ 69→ agent/ ADK-Go agent orchestrator, tools, and worker 70→ openai/ OpenAI-compatible LLM provider 71→ api/ REST API, SSE, middleware, auth 72→ db/ Connection pool and migrations 73→ ingestion/ Polling sources (Docker Hub, GitHub, ECR Public, GitLab, PyPI) 74→ models/ Shared domain types 75→ queue/ River job definitions and client 76→ routing/ Notification channels and delivery worker 77→web/ Next.js dashboard (React + Tailwind + shadcn) 78→scripts/ Integration test harness 79→``` 80→ 81→## Extending 82→ 83→More providers (npm, Helm, etc.) and channels (PagerDuty, etc.) are planned. Adding one is a single-interface implementation: 84→ 85→**Add a registry provider** — implement `IIngestionSource` in `internal/ingestion/source.go`: 86→ 87→```go 88→type IIngestionSource interface { 89→ FetchNewReleases(ctx context.Context) ([]IngestionResult, error) 90→} 91→``` 92→ 93→**Add a notification channel** — implement `Sender` in `internal/routing/sender.go`: 94→ 95→```go 96→type Sender interface { 97→ Send(ctx context.Context, channel models.NotificationChannel, msg Message) error 98→} 99→``` 100→ 101→## Useful commands 102→ 103→```bash 104→make build # go build -o changelogue ./cmd/server 105→make test # go test ./... 106→make vet # go vet ./... 107→make lint # alias for vet 108→make integration-test # full integration test (spins up its own Postgres) 109→make db-reset # drop and recreate the local database 110→make frontend-build # build Next.js static export 111→make agent-dev # run agent CLI for a specific project 112→make clean # remove binary, stop containers, delete volumes 113→``` 114→ Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.

data.prompt:
Find the README.md file and any existing code coverage configuration or badges. Also check if there's a Makefile with coverage commands. Look for:
1. README.md content
2. Any coverage-related files (.coveragerc, coverage.xml, etc.)
3. Makefile coverage targets
4. Any CI/CD config (.github/workflows) that runs coverage

content.content:
1→.PHONY: up down db-reset build run dev test vet lint \
     2→        frontend-install frontend-dev frontend-build \
     3→        integration-test agent-dev clean
     4→
     5→# --- Configuration ---
     6→DATABASE_URL ?= postgres://postgres:postgres@localhost:5432/changelogue?sslmode=disable
     7→LISTEN_ADDR  ?= :8080
     8→BINARY       := changelogue
     9→
    10→# --- Infrastructure ---
    11→up:
    12→	docker compose up -d
    13→	@echo "Waiting for Postgres to be healthy..."
    14→	@until docker compose exec -T postgres pg_isready -U postgres >/dev/null 2>&1; do sleep 1; done
    15→	@echo "Postgres is ready."
    16→
    17→down:
    18→	docker compose down
    19→
    20→db-reset:
    21→	docker compose exec -T postgres psql -U postgres -c "DROP DATABASE IF EXISTS changelogue;"
    22→	docker compose exec -T postgres psql -U postgres -c "CREATE DATABASE changelogue;"
    23→	@echo "Database reset."
    24→
    25→# --- Backend ---
    26→build:
    27→	go build -o $(BINARY) ./cmd/server
    28→
    29→run: build
    30→	DATABASE_URL="$(DATABASE_URL)" LISTEN_ADDR="$(LISTEN_ADDR)" NO_AUTH=true ./$(BINARY)
    31→
    32→test:
    33→	go test ./...
    34→
    35→vet:
    36→	go vet ./...
    37→
    38→lint: vet
    39→
    40→# --- Agent Dev ---
    41→agent-dev:
    42→	@if [ -z "$(PROJECT_ID)" ]; then echo "error: PROJECT_ID is required. Usage: make agent-dev PROJECT_ID=<uuid>"; exit 1; fi
    43→	DATABASE_URL="$(DATABASE_URL)" go run ./cmd/agent --project-id=$(PROJECT_ID) web api webui
    44→
    45→# --- Frontend ---
    46→frontend-install:
    47→	cd web && npm install
    48→
    49→frontend-dev:
    50→	cd web && npm run dev
    51→
    52→frontend-build:
    53→	cd web && npm run build
    54→
    55→# --- Integration ---
    56→integration-test:
    57→	bash scripts/integration-test.sh
    58→
    59→# --- Convenience ---
    60→dev: up run
    61→
    62→clean:
    63→	rm -f $(BINARY)
    64→	docker compose down -v
    65→

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

content.content:
1→name: CI
     2→
     3→on:
     4→  push:
     5→    branches: [main, master]
     6→  pull_request:
     7→    branches: [main, master]
     8→
     9→jobs:
    10→  backend:
    11→    name: Backend
    12→    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    13→    steps:
    14→      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
    15→
    16→      - uses: actions/setup-go@v5
    17→        with:
    18→          go-version-file: go.mod
    19→
    20→      - name: Vet
    21→        run: go vet ./...
    22→
    23→      - name: Build
    24→        run: go build -o changelogue ./cmd/server
    25→
    26→      - name: Test
    27→        run: go test ./...
    28→
    29→  frontend:
    30→    name: Frontend
    31→    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    32→    defaults:
    33→      run:
    34→        working-directory: web
    35→    steps:
    36→      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
    37→
    38→      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
    39→        with:
    40→          node-version: 20
    41→          cache: npm
    42→          cache-dependency-path: web/package-lock.json
    43→
    44→      - name: Install
    45→        run: npm ci
    46→
    47→      - name: Lint
    48→        run: npx eslint
    49→
    50→      - name: Build
    51→        run: npm run build
    52→
    53→  integration:
    54→    name: Integration
    55→    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    56→    steps:
    57→      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
    58→
    59→      - uses: actions/setup-go@v5
    60→        with:
    61→          go-version-file: go.mod
    62→
    63→      - name: Run integration tests
    64→        run: bash scripts/integration-test.sh
    65→

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

1→This is a [Next.js](https://nextjs.org) project bootstrapped with [`create-next-app`](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/cli/create-next-app).
     2→
     3→## Getting Started
     4→
     5→First, run the development server:
     6→
     7→```bash
     8→npm run dev
     9→# or
    10→yarn dev
    11→# or
    12→pnpm dev
    13→# or
    14→bun dev
    15→```
    16→
    17→Open [http://localhost:3000](http://localhost:3000) with your browser to see the result.
    18→
    19→You can start editing the page by modifying `app/page.tsx`. The page auto-updates as you edit the file.
    20→
    21→This project uses [`next/font`](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/building-your-application/optimizing/fonts) to automatically optimize and load [Geist](https://vercel.com/font), a new font family for Vercel.
    22→
    23→## Learn More
    24→
    25→To learn more about Next.js, take a look at the following resources:
    26→
    27→- [Next.js Documentation](https://nextjs.org/docs) - learn about Next.js features and API.
    28→- [Learn Next.js](https://nextjs.org/learn) - an interactive Next.js tutorial.
    29→
    30→You can check out [the Next.js GitHub repository](https://github.com/vercel/next.js) - your feedback and contributions are welcome!
    31→
    32→## Deploy on Vercel
    33→
    34→The easiest way to deploy your Next.js app is to use the [Vercel Platform](https://vercel.com/new?utm_medium=default-template&filter=next.js&utm_source=create-next-app&utm_campaign=create-next-app-readme) from the creators of Next.js.
    35→
    36→Check out our [Next.js deployment documentation](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/building-your-application/deploying) for more details.

Perfect! I have all the information needed. Here's my comprehensive report:

## Coverage Configuration Report for Changelogue

### 1. README.md Content
The main README at `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/README.md` includes:
- **Build badge** referencing CI workflow (`![Build](https://github.com/sentioxyz/releaseguard/actions/workflows/ci.yml/badge.svg)`)
- **AI Co-Authored badge** (no coverage badge currently)
- Useful commands section that lists: `make test`, `make vet`, `make lint`, and `make integration-test`
- **No code coverage badge or metrics displayed**

### 2. Makefile Coverage Targets
The `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/Makefile` contains these test-related targets:
```makefile
test:
	go test ./...

vet:
	go vet ./...

lint: vet

integration-test:
	bash scripts/integration-test.sh
```

**Key finding:** There are **no dedicated coverage targets** in the Makefile (no `make coverage`, `make test-coverage`, etc.)

### 3. CI/CD Configuration
The `.github/workflows/ci.yml` at `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/.github/workflows/ci.yml` has three jobs:
- **Backend**: Runs `go vet`, builds, and runs `go test ./...`
- **Frontend**: Installs deps, lints with eslint, and builds
- **Integration**: Runs bash integration tests

**Key finding:** No code coverage reporting, coverage upload step, or codecov integration present.

### 4. Coverage Files
**None found** at the repository root or in standard locations:
- No `.coveragerc`
- No `coverage.xml`
- No `.codecov.yml`
- No `codecov.yml`
- No coverage configuration files of any kind

## Summary

The Changelogue project currently has **NO code coverage infrastructure**:

| Item | Status |
|------|--------|
| README coverage badge | ❌ Not present |
| Makefile coverage target | ❌ Not present |
| CI/CD coverage reporting | ❌ Not present |
| Coverage config files | ❌ Not present |
| Codecov integration | ❌ Not present |

The project runs tests (`go test ./...`) in CI but does not measure, report, or track code coverage metrics.

Perfect! I have all the information needed. Here's my comprehensive report:

## Coverage Configuration Report for Changelogue

### 1. README.md Content
The main README at `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/README.md` includes:
- **Build badge** referencing CI workflow (`![Build](https://github.com/sentioxyz/releaseguard/actions/workflows/ci.yml/badge.svg)`)
- **AI Co-Authored badge** (no coverage badge currently)
- Useful commands section that lists: `make test`, `make vet`, `make lint`, and `make integration-test`
- **No code coverage badge or metrics displayed**

### 2. Makefile Coverage Targets
The `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/Makefile` contains these test-related targets:
```makefile
test:
	go test ./...

vet:
	go vet ./...

lint: vet

integration-test:
	bash scripts/integration-test.sh
```

**Key finding:** There are **no dedicated coverage targets** in the Makefile (no `make coverage`, `make test-coverage`, etc.)

### 3. CI/CD Configuration
The `.github/workflows/ci.yml` at `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/.github/workflows/ci.yml` has three jobs:
- **Backend**: Runs `go vet`, builds, and runs `go test ./...`
- **Frontend**: Installs deps, lints with eslint, and builds
- **Integration**: Runs bash integration tests

**Key finding:** No code coverage reporting, coverage upload step, or codecov integration present.

### 4. Coverage Files
**None found** at the repository root or in standard locations:
- No `.coveragerc`
- No `coverage.xml`
- No `.codecov.yml`
- No `codecov.yml`
- No coverage configuration files of any kind

## Summary

The Changelogue project currently has **NO code coverage infrastructure**:

| Item | Status |
|------|--------|
| README coverage badge | ❌ Not present |
| Makefile coverage target | ❌ Not present |
| CI/CD coverage reporting | ❌ Not present |
| Coverage config files | ❌ Not present |
| Codecov integration | ❌ Not present |

The project runs tests (`go test ./...`) in CI but does not measure, report, or track code coverage metrics.
Now let me run the tests with coverage to get the current metrics, and read the README.

input.description: Run tests with coverage to get current metrics

content.content:
1→

2→ 3→

4→ 5→![Build](https://github.com/sentioxyz/releaseguard/actions/workflows/ci.yml/badge.svg) 6→![AI Co-Authored](https://img.shields.io/endpoint?url=https://gist.githubusercontent.com/Poytr1/94cc8f0ddf90bb1d04dafbb76102786d/raw/ai-commits.json) 7→ 8→## What it does 9→ 10→- **Discovers releases** by polling Docker Hub, GitHub, ECR Public, GitLab, and PyPI on configurable intervals 11→- **Routes notifications** to Slack, Discord, email, and webhooks the moment a new version lands 12→- **Generates AI reports** via Google Gemini agents that research changelogs, assess risk, and summarize what changed 13→- **Serves a dashboard** (Next.js) for managing projects, sources, subscriptions, and browsing releases in real time 14→ 15→## Architecture 16→ 17→```mermaid 18→graph LR 19→ A["Ingestion
(polling)"] --> B["PostgreSQL +
River Queue"] 20→ B --> C["Agent
(ADK-Go)"] 21→ C --> D["Routing
(channels)"] 22→ 23→ A -.- A1["Docker Hub · GitHub
ECR · GitLab · PyPI"] 24→ B -.- B1["Transactional Outbox
LISTEN/NOTIFY → SSE"] 25→ C -.- C1["Gemini LLM
research tools"] 26→ D -.- D1["Slack · Discord
Email · Webhooks"] 27→``` 28→ 29→Release insert and job enqueue happen in a single SQL transaction — zero-loss guarantee. 30→ 31→See [ARCH.md](ARCH.md), [API.md](API.md), and [DESIGN.md](DESIGN.md) for the full design. 32→ 33→## Quick start 34→ 35→**Prerequisites:** Go 1.25+, Docker, Node.js 20+ 36→ 37→```bash 38→# Start Postgres and the server (NO_AUTH mode) 39→make dev 40→ 41→# In another terminal — start the frontend 42→make frontend-install 43→make frontend-dev 44→``` 45→ 46→The API runs on `localhost:8080`, the dashboard on `localhost:3000`. 47→ 48→## Environment variables 49→ 50→| Variable | Default | Purpose | 51→|----------|---------|---------| 52→| `DATABASE_URL` | `postgres://localhost:5432/changelogue?sslmode=disable` | PostgreSQL connection | 53→| `LISTEN_ADDR` | `:8080` | HTTP server bind address | 54→| `NO_AUTH` | _(unset)_ | Set to `true` to disable API key auth (development) | 55→| `LOG_LEVEL` | `info` | Log level: `debug`, `info`, `warn`, `error` | 56→| `LLM_PROVIDER` | `gemini` | LLM provider: `gemini` or `openai` | 57→| `LLM_MODEL` | `gemini-2.5-flash` / `gpt-5.2` | Model name (default depends on provider) | 58→| `GOOGLE_API_KEY` | _(empty)_ | Gemini API key (required when `LLM_PROVIDER=gemini`) | 59→| `OPENAI_API_KEY` | _(empty)_ | OpenAI API key (required when `LLM_PROVIDER=openai`) | 60→| `OPENAI_BASE_URL` | `https://api.openai.com/v1` | OpenAI-compatible API base URL | 61→ 62→## Project structure 63→ 64→``` 65→cmd/ 66→ server/ Entry point — wires all layers together 67→ agent/ Agent CLI — run agent analysis for a project 68→internal/ 69→ agent/ ADK-Go agent orchestrator, tools, and worker 70→ openai/ OpenAI-compatible LLM provider 71→ api/ REST API, SSE, middleware, auth 72→ db/ Connection pool and migrations 73→ ingestion/ Polling sources (Docker Hub, GitHub, ECR Public, GitLab, PyPI) 74→ models/ Shared domain types 75→ queue/ River job definitions and client 76→ routing/ Notification channels and delivery worker 77→web/ Next.js dashboard (React + Tailwind + shadcn) 78→scripts/ Integration test harness 79→``` 80→ 81→## Extending 82→ 83→More providers (npm, Helm, etc.) and channels (PagerDuty, etc.) are planned. Adding one is a single-interface implementation: 84→ 85→**Add a registry provider** — implement `IIngestionSource` in `internal/ingestion/source.go`: 86→ 87→```go 88→type IIngestionSource interface { 89→ FetchNewReleases(ctx context.Context) ([]IngestionResult, error) 90→} 91→``` 92→ 93→**Add a notification channel** — implement `Sender` in `internal/routing/sender.go`: 94→ 95→```go 96→type Sender interface { 97→ Send(ctx context.Context, channel models.NotificationChannel, msg Message) error 98→} 99→``` 100→ 101→## Useful commands 102→ 103→```bash 104→make build # go build -o changelogue ./cmd/server 105→make test # go test ./... 106→make vet # go vet ./... 107→make lint # alias for vet 108→make integration-test # full integration test (spins up its own Postgres) 109→make db-reset # drop and recreate the local database 110→make frontend-build # build Next.js static export 111→make agent-dev # run agent CLI for a specific project 112→make clean # remove binary, stop containers, delete volumes 113→``` 114→ Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.

file.content:
![Build](https://github.com/sentioxyz/releaseguard/actions/workflows/ci.yml/badge.svg) ![AI Co-Authored](https://img.shields.io/endpoint?url=https://gist.githubusercontent.com/Poytr1/94cc8f0ddf90bb1d04dafbb76102786d/raw/ai-commits.json) ## What it does - **Discovers releases** by polling Docker Hub, GitHub, ECR Public, GitLab, and PyPI on configurable intervals - **Routes notifications** to Slack, Discord, email, and webhooks the moment a new version lands - **Generates AI reports** via Google Gemini agents that research changelogs, assess risk, and summarize what changed - **Serves a dashboard** (Next.js) for managing projects, sources, subscriptions, and browsing releases in real time ## Architecture ```mermaid graph LR A["Ingestion
(polling)"] --> B["PostgreSQL +
River Queue"] B --> C["Agent
(ADK-Go)"] C --> D["Routing
(channels)"] A -.- A1["Docker Hub · GitHub
ECR · GitLab · PyPI"] B -.- B1["Transactional Outbox
LISTEN/NOTIFY → SSE"] C -.- C1["Gemini LLM
research tools"] D -.- D1["Slack · Discord
Email · Webhooks"] ``` Release insert and job enqueue happen in a single SQL transaction — zero-loss guarantee. See [ARCH.md](ARCH.md), [API.md](API.md), and [DESIGN.md](DESIGN.md) for the full design. ## Quick start **Prerequisites:** Go 1.25+, Docker, Node.js 20+ ```bash # Start Postgres and the server (NO_AUTH mode) make dev # In another terminal — start the frontend make frontend-install make frontend-dev ``` The API runs on `localhost:8080`, the dashboard on `localhost:3000`. ## Environment variables | Variable | Default | Purpose | |----------|---------|---------| | `DATABASE_URL` | `postgres://localhost:5432/changelogue?sslmode=disable` | PostgreSQL connection | | `LISTEN_ADDR` | `:8080` | HTTP server bind address | | `NO_AUTH` | _(unset)_ | Set to `true` to disable API key auth (development) | | `LOG_LEVEL` | `info` | Log level: `debug`, `info`, `warn`, `error` | | `LLM_PROVIDER` | `gemini` | LLM provider: `gemini` or `openai` | | `LLM_MODEL` | `gemini-2.5-flash` / `gpt-5.2` | Model name (default depends on provider) | | `GOOGLE_API_KEY` | _(empty)_ | Gemini API key (required when `LLM_PROVIDER=gemini`) | | `OPENAI_API_KEY` | _(empty)_ | OpenAI API key (required when `LLM_PROVIDER=openai`) | | `OPENAI_BASE_URL` | `https://api.openai.com/v1` | OpenAI-compatible API base URL | ## Project structure ``` cmd/ server/ Entry point — wires all layers together agent/ Agent CLI — run agent analysis for a project internal/ agent/ ADK-Go agent orchestrator, tools, and worker openai/ OpenAI-compatible LLM provider api/ REST API, SSE, middleware, auth db/ Connection pool and migrations ingestion/ Polling sources (Docker Hub, GitHub, ECR Public, GitLab, PyPI) models/ Shared domain types queue/ River job definitions and client routing/ Notification channels and delivery worker web/ Next.js dashboard (React + Tailwind + shadcn) scripts/ Integration test harness ``` ## Extending More providers (npm, Helm, etc.) and channels (PagerDuty, etc.) are planned. Adding one is a single-interface implementation: **Add a registry provider** — implement `IIngestionSource` in `internal/ingestion/source.go`: ```go type IIngestionSource interface { FetchNewReleases(ctx context.Context) ([]IngestionResult, error) } ``` **Add a notification channel** — implement `Sender` in `internal/routing/sender.go`: ```go type Sender interface { Send(ctx context.Context, channel models.NotificationChannel, msg Message) error } ``` ## Useful commands ```bash make build # go build -o changelogue ./cmd/server make test # go test ./... make vet # go vet ./... make lint # alias for vet make integration-test # full integration test (spins up its own Postgres) make db-reset # drop and recreate the local database make frontend-build # build Next.js static export make agent-dev # run agent CLI for a specific project make clean # remove binary, stop containers, delete volumes ```

research tools"] D -.- D1["Slack · Discord
Email · Webhooks"] ``` Release insert and job enqueue happen in a single SQL transaction — zero-loss guarantee. See [ARCH.md](ARCH.md), [API.md](API.md), and [DESIGN.md](DESIGN.md) for the full design. ## Quick start **Prerequisites:** Go 1.25+, Docker, Node.js 20+ ```bash # Start Postgres and the server (NO_AUTH mode) make dev # In another terminal — start the frontend make frontend-install make frontend-dev ``` The API runs on `localhost:8080`, the dashboard on `localhost:3000`. ## Environment variables | Variable | Default | Purpose | |----------|---------|---------| | `DATABASE_URL` | `postgres://localhost:5432/changelogue?sslmode=disable` | PostgreSQL connection | | `LISTEN_ADDR` | `:8080` | HTTP server bind address | | `NO_AUTH` | _(unset)_ | Set to `true` to disable API key auth (development) | | `LOG_LEVEL` | `info` | Log level: `debug`, `info`, `warn`, `error` | | `LLM_PROVIDER` | `gemini` | LLM provider: `gemini` or `openai` | | `LLM_MODEL` | `gemini-2.5-flash` / `gpt-5.2` | Model name (default depends on provider) | | `GOOGLE_API_KEY` | _(empty)_ | Gemini API key (required when `LLM_PROVIDER=gemini`) | | `OPENAI_API_KEY` | _(empty)_ | OpenAI API key (required when `LLM_PROVIDER=openai`) | | `OPENAI_BASE_URL` | `https://api.openai.com/v1` | OpenAI-compatible API base URL | ## Project structure ``` cmd/ server/ Entry point — wires all layers together agent/ Agent CLI — run agent analysis for a project internal/ agent/ ADK-Go agent orchestrator, tools, and worker openai/ OpenAI-compatible LLM provider api/ REST API, SSE, middleware, auth db/ Connection pool and migrations ingestion/ Polling sources (Docker Hub, GitHub, ECR Public, GitLab, PyPI) models/ Shared domain types queue/ River job definitions and client routing/ Notification channels and delivery worker web/ Next.js dashboard (React + Tailwind + shadcn) scripts/ Integration test harness ``` ## Extending More providers (npm, Helm, etc.) and channels (PagerDuty, etc.) are planned. Adding one is a single-interface implementation: **Add a registry provider** — implement `IIngestionSource` in `internal/ingestion/source.go`: ```go type IIngestionSource interface { FetchNewReleases(ctx context.Context) ([]IngestionResult, error) } ``` **Add a notification channel** — implement `Sender` in `internal/routing/sender.go`: ```go type Sender interface { Send(ctx context.Context, channel models.NotificationChannel, msg Message) error } ``` ## Useful commands ```bash make build # go build -o changelogue ./cmd/server make test # go test ./... make vet # go vet ./... make lint # alias for vet make integration-test # full integration test (spins up its own Postgres) make db-reset # drop and recreate the local database make frontend-build # build Next.js static export make agent-dev # run agent CLI for a specific project make clean # remove binary, stop containers, delete volumes ```

content.text:
Added a **Code coverage** section to the README (before "Useful commands") with a table of all packages sorted by coverage percentage. The data comes from `go test -cover ./...`:

| Package | Coverage |
|---------|----------|
| `internal/routing` | 81.6% |
| `internal/ingestion` | 61.5% |
| `internal/agent/openai` | 58.9% |
| `internal/api` | 37.2% |
| `internal/onboard` | 36.4% |
| `internal/auth` | 33.3% |
| `internal/agent` | 27.3% |
| `internal/queue` | 25.0% |

Packages with no test statements (`internal/models`) and packages that failed to build (`cmd/server`, `cmd/agent`, `internal/db`) were omitted.
